policy_statement: H.1: Anchor BIPOC families and advance their return to San Francisco through robust housing and stabilization programs.
mechanism_description: The city plans to use housing and stabilization programs (including affordable housing actions and tenant services) to support household stability, which can strengthen community resilience.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard → ↓ social vulnerability / ↓ displacement & exposure → ↓ climate impacts on people and communities
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human/system activity: The city funds or expands affordable housing, eviction prevention, tenant services, and related stabilization supports that reduce housing precarity and displacement.  
(2) Mediating physical/social process: Stable housing improves capacity to prepare for and withstand hazards (e.g., ability to shelter in place during heat/smoke, maintain medical/electric needs, receive warnings/aid, avoid post-disaster displacement), and strengthens social networks/service continuity that support recovery.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower morbidity/mortality and economic loss from climate extremes, faster recovery, and reduced disruption to community functioning under climate stressors.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing climate-hazard vulnerability and exposure by keeping households stably housed and better able to access support before/during/after extreme events.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Housing stabilization → reduced displacement/precariy → reduced vulnerability/exposure to climate hazards → reduced climate impacts (Adaptation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Any mitigation effects (e.g., density, reduced commuting) are indirect and not inherent to “stabilization/tenant services,” whereas vulnerability reduction is the stated and most consistent climate-relevant mechanism. It is not primarily Resource Efficiency (no direct “same service with less energy/material”) and not Nature-Based Solutions (no ecosystem/carbon sink pathway).  
3) Edge cases applied: Some housing programs can be mitigation if they explicitly drive building decarbonization or transit-oriented development; those are different mechanisms. This mechanism, as defined (stabilization/tenant services to strengthen resilience), maps most strongly to Adaptation.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Across contexts, housing stabilization aimed at community resilience consistently functions by reducing social vulnerability and displacement risk during climate stressors, fitting Adaptation broadly.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any secondary labeling (e.g., heat-health, disaster recovery, equity-focused adaptation) requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a “housing stabilization” program is implemented mainly through new construction patterns (e.g., compact infill reducing VMT) or mandates building electrification/efficiency, mitigation/resource-efficiency could become dominant; those cases should be treated as separate mechanisms or require policy-level reassessment.
key_indicators: “Anchor BIPOC families”; “advance their return to San Francisco”; “robust housing and stabilization programs”; “Supporting Actions”; “Leverage every housing action and investment”; “Prioritize affordable housing”; “Expand tenant services”; “increase housing production”
co_benefits: Equity/anti-displacement outcomes; improved public health and well-being; economic stability for households; potential reductions in homelessness
instance_edge_case_notes: High-level strategy language in a Climate Action Plan could also be implemented through infrastructure/investment or incentive programs, but the excerpt does not specify a concrete funding mechanism, requirement, or standard—so it is classified as a planning/strategy instrument.
